data.title: Billionaire former Tory donor pays himself £477m
data.description:
A billionaire former Tory donor paid himself £477m last year as revenues soared at his City hedge fund.

Chris Rokos cemented his position as one of Britain’s richest men, and is believed to be the highest-paid partner at his eponymous fund, Rokos Capital Management, which more than doubled its turnover from £445m to £1.2bn.

The fund’s latest accounts revealed that the total pool of profits for its 23 partners rose to a record £940m in the 12 months to March, up from £315m the year prior.

While the results do not disclose the identity of the highest-paid partner, Mr Rokos is widely considered the fund’s top earner, who founded the firm in 2015 and is its majority owner.

Mr Rokos, who is one of Britain’s leading hedge fund managers, was also previously a donor to the Conservatives, giving the party around £2m between 2009 and 2015. He made his last donation in 2018.

He also owns the centuries-old, Grade I listed Tottenham House, one of England’s grandest stately homes. Earlier this year, Mr Rokos made headlines by applying for planning permission to install around 1,100 solar panels to provide power to the 100-bed mansion.

Under the plans, two acres of the manor estate – currently used for silage and grazing dairy cows – would be developed to house solar panels. These would provide nearly one third of the power required to run the estate.

Tottenham House was part of the Savernake Estate, once a royal hunting forest with significant links to Henry VIII. The family of Jane Seymour, the King’s third wife, took ownership of the home before passing it down through several generations.

Mr Rokos has been working to restore the house into a family home.

He has launched a series of ambitious construction projects, such as underground tunnels connecting the main house to the stables. He has also installed a tennis pavilion and a cinema.

Before launching Rokos Capital, he also co-founded Brevan Howard, another City hedge fund.

Unlike most large hedge funds, which have multiple portfolio managers making bets on the market, Mr Rokos is the main trader at Rokos Capital.

He is supported by a team of analysts and senior investment professionals, which the firm calls investment officers.

Rokos Capital Management was contacted for comment.

data.title: Housebuilding giant blames Budget for biggest drop in sales since Covid
data.description:
Britain’s largest housebuilder has suffered the biggest drop in the number of homes sold since the pandemic after buyers were spooked by Rachel Reeves’s Budget.

Vistry, which owns brands such as Bovis and Countryside, sold 9.7pc fewer homes last year with sales falling to 15,700 from 17,225 in 2024.

Greg Fitzgerald, the company’s chief executive, said uncertainty over the November Budget had “delayed the timing” of some sales with “a more subdued market” triggered by Ms Reeves’s tax and spending plans.

The months leading to the Budget were dominated by speculation over the potential property taxes that the Chancellor would bring in to plug a multibillion-pound black hole in the public’s finances.

A so-called mansion tax on expensive homes will hit properties worth more than £2m with a tax surcharge from 2028 onwards.

Vistry’s woes will also prove a headache for Labour as it strives to hit its goal of building 1.5 million homes by the end of this parliament.

The Government has pledged to accelerate building plans to meet the target after a slow start left it behind schedule.

At Vistry, sales for future construction projects by the housebuilder – which teams up with housing associations and councils to build affordable homes – stand at around £4bn, compared with £4.4bn in the previous year.

The sales drop marks the largest decline since the depths of the Covid pandemic, when sales plunged by a third to 4,652 as lockdown wiped out the property market.

Shares in the housebuilder fell by nearly 8pc during trading, wiping off £52m.

However, the builder backed Labour’s affordable housing measures, which include billions of pounds of funding to boost partnership contracts over the next decade, saying it will buoy trading.

Mr Fitzgerald said: “Whilst market conditions remain uncertain in the near term, further benefits of our cost, productivity and mix enhancement initiatives will support the delivery of good year-on-year financial and strategic progress.”

It comes after Persimmon, another housebuilder, warned it did not expect “any material improvement” in the housing market this year despite the Government’s efforts.

The builder warned that inflation will remain high, while extra regulatory costs and landfill tax charges will slow down growth.

data.title: Trump warned credit card crackdown will trigger financial crisis for millions
data.description:
Donald Trump’s crackdown on credit cards risks pushing millions of poor Americans into financial crisis, some of Wall Street’s most powerful groups have warned.

Mr Trump is pressing ahead with plans to temporarily cap credit card interest rates for 12 months at 10pc from January 20.

However, the proposal is facing a backlash over fears it will force banks to cancel cards and pull back lending to consumers with the lowest credit scores.

On Monday, the American Bankers Association (ABA), which represents major lenders like JP Morgan, hedge fund billionaire Bill Ackman and the Bank Policy Institute (BPI) all rounded on Mr Trump over the plan.

Mr Ackman, who has publicly supported Mr Trump, added: “My concern about capping rates at 10pc is that doing so will inevitably cause millions of Americans to have their cards cancelled as credit card companies lose the ability to adequately price subprime credit risk.

“Consumers denied credit cards will be forced to turn to loan sharks whose rates and terms will be vastly worse for borrowers.”

The BPI said the cap would “be devastating for millions of American families and small business owners … the very consumers this proposal intends to help”. The ABA said the move would push consumers towards “less regulated, more costly alternatives”.

Mr Trump’s announcement sent shares in US credit card providers sliding on Monday, with Capital One leading the fallers plunging by as much as 6.24pc.

In the UK, as much as £8bn was wiped off the market value of British banks. Shares in Barclays, which has a US credit card business, fell by 3.3pc in London. Shares in NatWest were also down by 1.2pc, even though the bank does not operate a US credit card business.

The plan has stoked widespread fears because credit cards are so vital for the US economy, with the number of accounts hitting a record 642 million in the third quarter of 2025.

This means the US has nearly twice as many credit card accounts as it does people. Total credit card debt is at a record $1.23tn (£891bn) – up by 39pc since the pandemic began.

In theory, capping at 10pc should dramatically reduce borrowers’ costs because it would more than halve the average rate paid, which was 22.5pc in November.

At no point since at least 1994, when Federal Reserve data began, has the rate been as low as 10pc. According to Vanderbilt University, a 10pc cap would save US consumers $73bn net a year.

However, analysts warn it is precisely the people who Trump is trying to help that will be hit the hardest by his rate cap because it will force banks to do less lending.

Justin Begley, economist at Moody’s Analytics said: “If the government does indeed enforce this policy on the banks, then it’s just going to become completely unprofitable to lend to people that are of higher credit risk.

“I would disagree with people who are saying that the banks can absorb this. The banks have to be able to properly price credit risk.”

Banks may simply cancel subprime borrowers’ credit cards outright, Mr Begley added. “That’s something I have heard banks talking about doing. They’re going to want to protect themselves.”

Jefferies economist Mohit Kumar said: “It is likely that lower rates will push credit providers to reassess their lending criteria and the policy ends up creating more harm than good.”

Subprime borrowers have a credit card default rate of 1.2pc, double the overall default rate, according to Moody’s. Currently, subprime borrowers make up 21pc of accounts in default. During the financial crisis, the rate was closer to half.

The measure will hit at a time when low and middle income US households are becoming increasingly overwhelmed with their debts.

The share of Americans who are falling seriously behind on their credit card payments has already soared to a near 15-year high.

Debt advice firms have been warning that America’s cost of living crisis is increasingly hitting middle class households, as families turn to credit just to cover the costs of basic necessities.

data.title: The towns where the taps ran dry
data.description:
On a grey Tuesday in East Grinstead, a little girl runs along the pavement to the redbrick town library. She stands at the automatic glass doors waiting for them to open, but nothing happens. A moment later her mother arrives. The woman looks at the sign that reads “library closed today”, then tells the child she will not be able to borrow any books after all.

Like almost every business or public building in this West Sussex town, the library is shut as a result of South East Water’s failure to keep the taps running.

The company’s bosses might just about be forgiven if this were a one-off. But it is the second officially declared major incident in South East Water territory in as many months, with customers in Tunbridge Wells, Kent, still facing disruption after weeks of problems.

South East has repeatedly fallen short on its most basic duty. In 2023, 6,000 households in Kent and Sussex went without water when supplies at South East ran out. In 2022, thousands of people spent Christmas with no water due to leakages.

The company, which serves 2.3 million customers, is now well on its way to claiming the crown of Britain’s worst utility from basket-case Thames Water, leaving its licence to operate under threat.

Businesses on the brink

Dave Hinton, the chief executive of South East Water, who took home £515,000 last year including a £115,000 bonus, was hauled in front of a parliamentary committee earlier this month. He had been summoned to answer questions on the crisis that hit Tunbridge Wells in December and ruined Christmas for 24,000 homes and businesses.

The spa town made an unlikely backdrop for a water crisis. From the 17th century, Tunbridge Wells’s mineral waters drew bathers and wealth.

But in the 21st century, water dealt an economic blow. Pub and bar owners in Tunbridge Wells told The Telegraph they had lost ruinous amounts of money.

“In four days we lost between £15,000 to £20,000. We are really struggling,” Bianca Omer, the manager of the cocktail bar Rocca said.

The bar survived. But Tudor Price, the chief executive of Kent’s chamber of commerce, believes that pubs, restaurants and bars in Tunbridge Wells “potentially lost about £20m of revenue” as a result of the outage.

Some are now on the brink. With many pubs already threatened by the Chancellor’s business rates raid, lengthy closure may deliver the final blow for some in Tunbridge Wells.

More alarmingly, a kidney treatment centre, 19 care homes, and two hospitals were left without water too.

‘Flying blind’

Debate has raged over whether the crisis in Tunbridge Wells was foreseeable. Hinton, perhaps unsurprisingly, reckons not.

At his parliamentary hearing he told Alistair Carmichael, the no-nonsense MP for Orkney and chairman of the environment select committee, that the whole thing was a bolt from the blue. In fact, because the crisis came as such a shock, Hinton insisted, he’d give himself “eight out of 10 in terms of response”.

The A-grade mark, as Carmichael put it, raised eyebrows among committee members. But when Marcus Rink, the boss of the Drinking Water Inspectorate, delivered his verdict, the benefit of the doubt evaporated.

“It shouldn’t have been a surprise,” Rink said. The water treatment works at nearby Pembury hadn’t just gone down for a few hours – it had been failing for some time, he explained.

“The start of the incident, in the opinion of my inspectors, was Nov 9.” With the Pembury plant the only water treatment works serving Tunbridge Wells, its continued good health is essential to the area.

Rink said South East was “flying blind”. The plant lacked electronic monitoring, relying on manual observations that missed failing filters.

Despite prior regulatory enforcement notices and missing micro-filtration units, the company had ignored weeks of visible deterioration, eventually leading to a totally avoidable system collapse.

The picture for South East became worse as Rink laid out more details. The company had been notified that there were issues with the treatment works a year previously. Testing had been intermittent, and the specialists monitoring the facility had all knocked off on Friday afternoon before returning on Monday morning. Nov 9 was a Sunday.

If MPs have been determined to hold Hinton to account, the same cannot be said of South East Water’s non-executive directors. Other managers have been sent out to face the music, among them a flustered Neil Hudson, South East’s head of water quality.

“What would happen to people if they do drink it?” a local BBC news reporter demanded to know after residents were advised to boil their water before using it.

“Any sort of normal, healthy individual will probably see no impact whatsoever. If anyone drinks the water and starts to feel unwell, our advice would be to speak to your GP,” Hudson said.

Asked by MPs why he had dodged the media, Hinton claimed journalists would not have asked relevant questions. They would have focused on his salary and South East Water’s finances, he complained.

When he finally came out of hiding, Hinton told ITV that he was “an operational CEO” and as such had spent the crisis participating in “traditional CEO activities”.

Henry Tuffnell, the young Labour MP for Pembrokeshire who sits on the environment committee, pointed out that for an “operational CEO” Hinton seemed to be remarkably short of operational detail.

Tuffnell tells The Telegraph: “You can’t say you were all over the detail, and therefore didn’t have time to do any media, if you weren’t over the detail.”

Even as Hinton faced MPs, back in Tunbridge Wells a second major incident was already in the offing. Still recovering from the pre-Christmas outage, locals were cut off again on Saturday.

Round two, according to South East, was due to “recent cold weather and a subsequent breakout of leaks and bursts”. This was no minor blip. Within days, 30,000 homes and businesses across Kent and Sussex had no water at all.

‘We have had enough’

In East Grinstead, four schoolboys kick a yellow football against the wall. They are the only people in the town who seem to think the outage is a good thing. “It’s a day off,” one of them offers cheerfully.

In Waitrose, almost all of the water has gone from the shelves except for a few bottles of San Pellegrino and some strawberry-flavoured Volvic. There was a water station in the car park the previous day, but it closed after 90 minutes.

East Grinstead council said: “We were not contacted directly by South East Water at any stage during the recent water supply disruption in the town. To the best of the council’s understanding, local knowledge was not utilised when setting up the initial water distribution stations.

“The town council regrets that it was not involved from the outset, as early engagement and local knowledge could have helped ensure a smoother, safer, and more coordinated response for the town’s residents.”

Local knowledge is clearly not South East Water’s strong suit. In December the company began its comedy of errors in Tunbridge Wells with the opening of a water station in Tonbridge, seven miles away.

For those affected, it’s hard to see the funny side. Gary, who lives homeless in East Grinstead, and had just two bottles of water left, told The Telegraph he was “scared”. Like many in the town, he wondered: “What is this all about?”

Jonathan Hawker, a Tunbridge Wells resident who set up the campaign group Dry Wells Action, says: “We can’t wash, flush toilets, do the dishes, wash clothes, top up boilers – all the normal stuff you take for granted – because of this abject failure by South East Water.

“We have had enough. We will now fight for our right to water. Tunbridge Wells is no longer disgusted. We are absolutely furious.”

Heavy hitters

South East’s board is now facing serious questions. Chris Train, the chairman, has been no more forthcoming about appearing publicly than his chief executive.

Described on the company’s website as bringing “a lot of experience”, he is also chairman of Sellafield, which in 2024 was fined for serious cyber security failings. The Commons public accounts committee criticised the nuclear power plant last year for “failure, cost-overruns and continuing safety concerns”.

After Hinton’s select committee mauling, Train offered a review by one of the non-executive directors.

Committee chairman Alistair Carmichael responded: “Members of the press and public would be forgiven for seeing Mr Train’s announcement of a further review of the Pembury failure as an attempt to buy themselves time, to hunker down until this storm blows over.

“They also assert that their review will be ‘independent’. It stretches the meaning of ‘independent’ when their review is to be conducted by a member of its board, supported by its staff. What was the investigation done by the Drinking Water Inspectorate if it was not an independent review?”

Hinton’s furious phone call

Desperate Tunbridge Wells residents have called for the Army to be brought in. Though that may sound a little over the top, there is logic to the request.

Hawker says: “The Army are logistics specialists who are used to dealing with challenging situations. We need experts who can manage logistics in emergency situations.”

South East Water officials can’t even manage a basic task like carting bottles of water from one place to another, he argues.

If Hinton has been the baddie, Mike Martin, the Liberal Democrat MP for Tunbridge Wells, has emerged as something of a local hero. He has had his sleeves rolled up throughout the process and repeatedly taken Hinton to task. This resulted in Hinton phoning Martin one evening during the eye of the storm to give him both barrels.

In his evidence to the environment committee, Martin said Hinton had accused him of politicising the crisis, asking: “How can you sleep at night?” The chief executive admitted to Carmichael that the telephone tantrum had not been his “finest moment”.

South East is more used to getting away with it.

As well as the four major shortages customers have endured on Hinton’s watch, South East Water has ranked in the bottom three companies for disruptions every year since Hinton took over. In 2023, it had the dubious accolade of recording the most interruptions and outages in England and Wales.

Its performance is getting worse, not better. In 2025, just 13 of it 38 annual targets were met – a little more than a third. Among the areas where it fell short were leakages, supply interruptions, water taste and odour, customer satisfaction and value for money. The previous two years, South East scored 15 – equivalent to less than 40pc. In 2022, it ranked positively on 24 of the 38 goals.

The company has suggested that its systems would be much better if only it had been able to raise customers’ bills, a familiar refrain in the water industry.

Yet it already has. Bills went up by 20pc just last year, meaning the average customer now pays £294 a year. It is also one of only five companies pushing to lift bills above the figure set by the regulator over the next five years.

Ofwat also gave short shrift to South East’s most recent investment plan. The company’s performance commitments were “of low to moderate ambition and do not go far enough in some cases”, the regulator said last April.

When it comes to “water supply interruptions, per capita consumption, repairs to burst mains and biodiversity”, the targets South East Water proposed “remained among the lowest across the sector”, Ofwat added.

South East responded by accusing the watchdog of being “unfair, disproportionate and punitive”. The company boasts of having “60 teams repairing leaks reactively and 86 leakage technicians and analysts searching for and finding leaks proactively”. More than 25,000 leaks were repaired in 2024, it says.

For any customers who feel they’re not getting value for money, the company points out: “The average drinking water bill now costs 81p per day, less than the cost of a second-class stamp.”

A spokesman said: “Our priority is improving South East Water in the future. We are strategically focused on our business plan and committed to delivering the infrastructure investment needed. This will improve our resilience as we face the challenges of climate change and population growth.”

It adds that Pembury’s water treatment works at the centre of the Tunbridge Wells crisis is due a full upgrade: “In advance of this full refurbishment, a number of improvements and changes are being made. Some of these are complete, and others are still ongoing, all of which are in line with the regulators’ timescales.”

Finances are underwater

South East’s financial performance is no less wretched than its operational performance. The company has failed to post a profit for the last three years, and though pre-tax losses roughly halved to £19.7m last year, they total £130m since 2022.

Weighed down with £1.3bn of borrowings – a figure that has steadily increased every year under Hinton – the cost of servicing those debts regularly eclipses the company’s profits. In the last five years, more than £400m has gone out of the door on debt costs and finance charges alone.

A further £168m has been paid out dividends, £136m of it in the form of a regulator-approved repayment of an internal loan to its parent company in 2020. South East Water is ultimately owned by a consortium comprising Australian and Canadian funds and NatWest’s pension fund.

In the two-year period from 2020 to 2022, the amount that South East paid out in dividends and debt interest surpassed what it spent upgrading infrastructure.

In 2024, the company revealed that it needed further cash to stay afloat. Ten months later, it received a £200m equity injection from shareholders.

Yet none of this has stopped Hinton receiving hundreds of thousands of pounds in pay and bonuses every year. Total earnings since assuming the top job in 2020 stand at an estimated £2.3m. Last year, he was paid a basic salary of £307,000, a £115,000 bonus and received a further £35,000 in pension contributions and other benefits.

A spokesman for South East said: “South East Water remains committed to a remuneration framework that supports a performance culture, and recognises success but does not reward poor performance.

“The bonus scheme is evaluated by the company’s independent non-executive director-led remuneration committee. The executive directors are not part of this committee and have no say in their remuneration.

“We can confirm that no bonus payment was made for operational performance for the year 2024-25. Details of all payments made can be found in our annual report on our website.”

‘Out of control’

Yet pinning the fiasco on a lack of investment when executives’ backs are against the wall doesn’t wash with Tunbridge Wells MP Mike Martin.

“Quite clearly, corporate governance has broken down,” he said. “We are now in a situation where a major public utility is out of control – failing its customers, risking public health and treating Parliament with contempt.”

If the board won’t step up, Martin thinks the utility’s bosses have to get involved. NatWest’s pension scheme owns 25pc of South East Water.

“NatWest Group Pension Fund will use its influence as a minority shareholder to direct South East Water’s board to ensure these issues are fully resolved,” it said in response to a letter from Martin this week.

The regulator has been spurred to action too. On Wednesday evening, the Government finally showed some mettle by calling on Ofwat to review South East Water’s licence to operate.

In an uncharacteristic show of speediness, on Thursday Ofwat announced an investigation into whether the supplier had met its obligations and offered support to those affected. Ofwat sceptics point out the regulator has been investigating South East since 2023.

There are local suspicions that Labour have, to a degree, let the crisis escalate because Tunbridge Wells and East Grinstead will never turn red. Richard Tice, the deputy leader of Reform UK, told The Telegraph that’s overthinking it. He said: “The problem is utter management incompetence at South East Water. My hunch is cock-up, not conspiracy.”

Yet the crisis is ongoing, and the inquisition is just getting started. By the water station at East Grinstead sports club, where the cars are backed up as far as you can see and people are pushing shopping trolleys full of bottles back home, a little boy on a bike asks if you can drink the water from the pond.

data.title: BBC strikes deal to make bespoke TV shows for YouTube
data.description:
The BBC has struck a landmark deal to make shows for YouTube as it grapples with an exodus of viewers to the streaming service.

The public service broadcaster will begin making programmes specifically for YouTube under the terms of a deal that could be announced as early as next week, the Financial Times reported.

These programmes, which would primarily be aimed at younger viewers, would subsequently be shown on the corporation’s own streaming platforms iPlayer and Sounds.

The BBC is seeking to face up to the existential threat posed by YouTube, which is increasingly drawing viewers away from traditional TV.

Figures from the official ratings agency Barb this week revealed YouTube has now overtaken the BBC for audience reach for the first time in history.

Almost 52 million people watched YouTube on their televisions, smartphones or laptops in December, compared with the 50.8 million Britons who tuned in to the BBC.

More people also watched YouTube than BBC channels in October and November, signalling the end of the corporation’s dominance of British airwaves for the last 90 years.

Last year, Ofcom urged the BBC and other traditional broadcasters to make programming specifically for YouTube, warning that they risked fading into irrelevance as younger viewers increasingly shunned conventional TV channels.

The corporation has previously used the streaming platform solely for trailers and clips, with audiences only able to view its shows on iPlayer – though senior executives have recently acknowledged the need to change this approach.

The BBC is now expected to develop new formats to be shown on YouTube. This is said to include programming specifically designed to combat the spread of misinformation online.

Alongside shows aimed at younger viewers, the BBC’s YouTube output will also include children’s programming and sports-related content. Some older series may also be shown on the platform, though this is not said to be a focus of the deal.

Advertising revenue

In addition to luring in defecting audiences, the YouTube deal will allow the BBC to generate more advertising revenues from shows viewed outside the UK.

Tim Davie, the BBC’s outgoing director-general, has long emphasised the need to bolster the corporation’s commercial offering to offset a loss of licence fee revenues.

Income from the licence fee has dropped by 30pc in real terms over the last decade owing to government-imposed freezes, while the BBC lost more than £1bn last year from people evading or cancelling the licence fee, which is expected to rise from £174.50 to £181 in April.

It comes at a crucial time for the BBC, which is entering discussions with the Government about the future of the licence fee before the end of the current charter period in 2027.

Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, has said she will consider a range of potential reforms including replacing the levy with advertising or subscriptions.

The BBC is also fighting a $10bn (£7.5bn) lawsuit filed by Donald Trump over a misleading edit in an episode of Panorama, which was first reported by The Telegraph.

Lawyers for the broadcaster have asked a judge to dismiss the suit, arguing that the Florida court venue is “improper” and that the US president has failed to show the documentary caused him harm.

The partnership is the latest example of traditional broadcasters seeking collaboration and even consolidation with streaming rivals.

Last summer ITV struck a deal with Disney that will see the two companies carry each other’s programming on their respective streaming platforms.

ITV is also locked in discussions about a £1.6bn takeover by Sky owner Comcast, with executives arguing that greater scale is needed to compete with the likes of Netflix and YouTube.

The BBC declined to comment. YouTube has been contacted for comment.

data.title: Thames Water lenders prepared to take £7bn hit to avoid nationalisation
data.description:
A group of Thames Water creditors is prepared to effectively write off £7.5bn of debt in a bid to avoid nationalisation.

The consortium has proposed a turnaround plan that would cancel around a third of Thames Water’s near-£20bn debt pile and commit the group to investing in the company.

Outstanding fines would be paid – but the investor group, dubbed London & Valley Water, would also seek leniency on future penalties in exchange for investment.

Thames Water revealed over the summer that annual losses had hit £1.6bn as a soaring debt burden and continued sewage failures fuelled its financial crisis.

Bosses said it would take at least a decade to turn around Britain’s largest water company.

The company’s creditors are looking to secure backing for their plans to avoid the utility being put into a temporary special administration regime, which would effectively wipe out their investments.

Lenders said they would not pay a dividend while conducting their turnaround plans or until the company is listed.

They also committed not to sell the business before the end of March 2030.

The consortium said the “more ambitious” plan would increase investment in Thames Water by around £1bn compared to proposals they submitted to Ofwat in May.

The latest proposal was the result of “three months of detailed discussion” with the water regulator and would “support long-term financial resilience and is intended to help the company pay for outstanding fines”, it added.

Mike McTighe, a City veteran whose current roles include chairman of Telegraph Media Group, would become chairman of Thames Water under the creditors’ proposals.

He said: “There is a huge amount of work to be done to turn around Thames Water and deliver the improved service and environmental outcomes that customers and local communities deserve.

“From day one, we will inject billions in new investment, strengthen Thames Water’s balance sheet, transform the company for thousands of hard-working frontline staff and begin the delivery of an operational turnaround that puts 16 million customers and the environment first.”

Lenders of Thames Water’s “Class A” debt would receive at least 10pc of new equity in the utility company in exchange for writing off about 25pc of their stake, worth around £4bn.

Thames Water said it remains in discussions over the bid but any agreement would be subject to High Court approval.

Chris Weston, the chief executive of Thames Water, said: “Today is an important milestone in our ongoing work with creditors and stakeholders to secure a market-led recapitalisation that establishes the financial and regulatory foundations required to support the investment and performance improvements our customers expect.”

data.title: Bidding war rises over weight loss drug that could prevent ‘Ozempic face’
data.description:
Two of the world’s largest pharmaceutical companies are locked in a bidding war for the maker of a weight-loss drug that could help patients avoid succumbing to “Ozempic face”.

Danish giant Novo Nordisk has launched an attempt to gatecrash Pfizer’s $7.3bn (£5.6bn) takeover of US biotech Metsera by tabling an offer of up to $9bn.

Metsera, which had already agreed to the Pfizer deal, described the approach from Novo Nordisk as “superior”.

However, Pfizer hit back by branding the offer as “reckless”. The US pharmaceutical giant now has four working days to salvage a deal.

It comes as drug giants scramble to secure the newest and most innovative weight-loss treatments to meet soaring demand.

Metsera is developing treatments based on the hormone amylin, which researchers have suggested could stop people from losing muscle during rapid weight loss.

Currently, Eli Lilly’s Mounjaro and Novo Nordisk’s Wegovy help people to shed pounds, but also lead them to lose muscle as well as fat.

The same goes for Novo’s Ozempic, which is a diabetes drug used off-label for weight loss.

‘Ozempic face’

As well as being praised for the weight-loss credentials, the drugs have also been blamed for the rise of “Ozempic face”. This is where people receiving treatment start to appear gaunt as they lose muscle mass in their cheeks.

However, researchers have suggested that amylin could help people to maintain muscle even as they lose weight, as the hormone appears to play a role in maintaining body composition.

Dimitris Papamargaritis, an associate professor at the University of Leicester, earlier this year said: “Early lab studies suggest that adding amylin could help people lose more fat while sparing muscle, though this still needs confirmation in humans.”

Pharmaceutical companies have become increasingly aware of the issue as the drugs grow in popularity.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the AstraZeneca chief executive, last year said weight-loss medicines needed to be more targeted towards fat to help people avoid losing too much muscle in the long term.

He said: “Today you lose weight but you lose fat and you lose muscle. The problem with many of [the treatments] is when you stop, you regain weight, because obesity is a chronic disease.

“Most people, as soon as they stop taking the medicines, they regain fat, but not so much the muscle that they have lost unless, of course, they go to the gym.”

AstraZeneca is developing its own weight-loss treatments, with the aim of leapfrogging rivals with new, potentially cheaper, obesity drugs. In 2023, it bought a Chinese biotech which was working on a weight-loss pill.

Metsera is also developing pill-based weight-loss drugs, as well as longer-lasting treatments. It is testing one injection that people would only have to take once a month, compared to current weight-loss jabs that require once-a-week injections.

Novo Nordisk made the offer for the US biotech as it battles a slump in performance, having recently announced plans to cut 9,000 jobs.

data.title: Electrical equipment shortage threatens net zero, Hitachi warns Miliband
data.description:
A shortage of electrical equipment threatens to derail Ed Miliband’s flagship net zero targets, a leading manufacturer has warned.

Hitachi, the world’s leading producer of high-voltage transformers, said a critical lack of kit could leave UK power operators unable to expand the grid fast enough to achieve clean electricity by 2030.

Laura Fleming, Hitachi’s UK managing director, said this shortfall was caused by unprecedented demand for electricity combined with a shortage of raw materials and skilled labour.

This has led to soaring prices and delayed deliveries for electrical equipment crucial to net zero.

Ms Fleming said: “In the UK and Europe, we’ve had 30-40 years of growing these [electrical] systems only on an as-needed basis and we’ve actually not seen electricity consumption go up dramatically.

“But we are now in a very different place where we’ve got very ageing systems with ageing equipment that needs replacing.

“At the same time, we’re trying to expand that grid because we’re moving from an energy system that is predominantly oil and gas-based to an energy system that is much more based on renewables, nuclear and a handful of other technologies.

“This is creating pressure on the system in a number of ways.”

The scale of the planned changes to Britain’s energy system is huge, inspired by the Energy Secretary’s move to embrace green electricity and slash the country’s reliance on fossil fuels.

Currently, the UK’s power stations, wind farms and other electricity producers have a total generating capacity of about 100 gigawatts.

This comes from a diverse mix of generators, ranging from massive gas-fired or nuclear power stations that produce steady, reliable power to intermittent wind and solar farms dependent on the weather.

Transformers are the unseen technology that unifies this system, converting the power they produce into the standardised voltages and frequencies needed by the UK’s power networks.

They work by “stepping up” power to the high voltages needed to efficiently transmit it long distances, typically around 400,000 volts.

Then, at the other end of the line, they reduce it back down to the 240 volts we need to run our homes.

But the 500 or so supergrid transformers that form the backbone of Britain’s high-voltage power transmission system are no longer enough to meet the nation’s needs.

Mostly installed between five and six decades ago, their age is showing – as highlighted by the catastrophic substation fire that shut down Heathrow last year, subsequently traced to a faulty 60-year-old transformer.

There are also far too few of them to support Mr Miliband’s ambitions of decarbonising the entire grid by 2030, along with a significant increase in power generation by 2050.

He has pledged to displace the oil and gas that now supply over 75pc of our total energy with green electricity from wind, solar and nuclear plants.

Such promises are easy to make but require mammoth upgrades to Britain’s grid.

Transformer capacity is measured in megavolt-amperes (MVA), with the UK currently boasting a capacity of about 170,000MVA.

By 2035, that will have to reach up to 280,000MVA – a two thirds increase – with even more needed by 2050.

That’s an unprecedented increase – and Britain’s problem is that its plans to expand and update its transmission network have coincided with everyone else’s across Europe.

The UK has three transmission operators: National Grid covering England and Wales, Scottish Power covering southern Scotland and SSE covering northern Scotland.

Recently, all three have found themselves pitched primarily against Tennet, the Dutch-German grid operator, for power transformers, subsea cables and other vital equipment.

For example, Britain wants to connect 21GW of offshore windfarms to its grid while Tennet has its own plans for 40GW of new wind.

The resulting power struggle has seen transformer order times surge from six months to four years, with prices rising 75pc in real terms since 2019.

Ms Fleming said it’s not just demand for raw materials that is driving up costs but the risk involved and the debt needed to finance new factories.

“Prices have gone up across the piece but it’s not just the costs for materials that have gone up,” she said. “It’s also the cost of finance. From a developer’s point of view, that probably adds more cost than the overall commodity costs.”

A 2025 report from the International Energy Agency set out the scale of the problem.

“Transformer lead times have almost doubled over the past two to three years,” it said. “Manufacturers have reported a record backlog of orders, increasing by one third in just one year.”

Artificial intelligence’s role

Hitachi is just one of several major players but its transformer backlog has now risen to $43bn (£32bn) – a trend being accelerated by the rise of AI.

Ms Fleming points to an internal Hitachi report – “Gridlocked? AI’s energy appetite” – which quantifies the impact of AI on the demand for transformers and associated equipment such as cables.

It calculates that Europe, including the UK, will need up to 200 giant new transformers by 2030 just for its new data centres, while America will install up to 460 and China another 130.

Add in the rest of the world and the total comes to 1,400 new transformers by 2030, just for AI alone.

Ms Fleming said this means that Britain faces a triple challenge.

It has to upgrade its old grid, expand it to meet the demands of decarbonisation and then expand it again for the needs of AI.

That’s a challenge but it could also be an opportunity – a chance for Britain to lead the world in manufacturing the parts needed for a future powered by green electricity. Will Britain grab that opportunity?

Serving the UK from Europe

Last month, regulator Ofgem announced its final approval for £90bn of investments proposed by National Grid, SSE and Scottish Power in pylons, substations and transformers, with the costs added to household and business energy bills.

So far, however, it’s clear that most of the kit needed to upgrade and expand the grid will be made abroad.

Ms Fleming said that most of the Hitachi transformers deployed in the UK will come from its factory near Łódź in Poland, assembled from components made in countries such as Sweden, Spain and Finland.

Meanwhile, the reluctance of investors like Hitachi to invest in the UK is largely related to its turbulent politics, especially around net zero.

For example, until the 2024 election, the target for decarbonising the grid was 2035 – a date chosen because of the long lead times needed for such massive infrastructure projects.

When Mr Miliband moved that date to 2030 after Labour came to power in July 2024, it prompted a radical political shift.

Notably, it destroyed the decades-long political consensus on climate in Westminster, a reversal that has seen the Conservatives row back from green policies that they introduced.

It also further opened the door to Reform, which has pledged to abolish net zero targets altogether.

In an interview last spring, Ms Fleming said that, faced with such turbulence, Hitachi could not take the risk of investing in UK factories.

“What we require beyond 2030 to invest in the UK is longer-term visibility,” she said. “So the visibility until 2030 is great but to be able to start considering longer-term investment, that visibility needs to go well beyond the political cycle.”

Such concerns surrounding the UK are already costing swathes of jobs and hundreds of millions of pounds in investment.

At Hitachi’s transformer factory in Sweden, for example, the company has doubled the workforce from 4,000 to 8,000 – about 1,000 new jobs a year, many to build transformers for the UK.

As Bruno Melles, Hitachi’s head of transformer manufacturing, puts it: “Right now, we believe that we can serve Great Britain from the investment that we made in Europe.

“The major factories supplying Britain are in Poland, Sweden, Germany, Spain and Italy. I have to say, all our factories are very, very active to ensure that we can supply Great Britain in a proper way – from Europe.”

A Government spokesman said: “Upgrading our electricity networks after years of underinvestment is essential to keep the lights on and ensure energy security for our country.

“We work closely with transmission operators who have robust long-term supply plans to secure essential components like transformers and with industry to make the most of Great British Energy’s £1bn plan to strengthen UK clean energy supply chains.”

data.title: ​Oliver Marriott, author of The Property Boom who restored the Yorkshire manor house Grimston Garth
data.description:
​Oliver Marriott​, who has died aged 86, was a financial journalist and property company director who restored an exceptional East Yorkshire mansion.

In his early career, Marriott was property editor of Investors’ Chronicle, financial editor of The Times and the author of well-praised investigative books, The Property Boom (1967) and Anatomy of a Merger (1970).

The former shone light on the machinations of new-rich tycoons in a fast-moving market, ruffling feathers and attracting several legal threats. The latter (co-authored with Robert Jones) explored the amalgamation of the ambitious General Electric Co led by Arnold Weinstock with the more traditional Associated Electrical Industries and English Electric to form a British industrial titan of its era.

In 1969, Marriott left journalism to become a property player in his own right. He joined forces with Jeffrey (later Lord) Sterling and Bruce McPhail in Sterling Guarantee Trust, acquired from the Slater Walker investment group, which they rapidly expanded by acquiring lame-duck companies with attractive assets – Marriott bringing depth of knowledge as the in-house m​arket analyst.

The 1970 redevelopment of the Gamages department store site in Oxford Street was an early success.

The shrewd Sterling operation survived the crash of 1974 to emerge, with Bank of England and lenders’ support, as the rescuer of the much larger Town & City group – the sector’s biggest potential casualty as owner of Arndale shopping centres and other commercial properties valued at their peak at £600 million. Later, Sterling Guarantee merged with the shipping and property giant P&O, where Marriott was a director until 1991.

Meanwhile, in 1972 Marriott had bought Grimston Garth, a late-18th-century Gothic manor house designed by John Carr of York on a triangular footprint around a central hexagon, with castellated corner towers, in the Holderness district of East Yorkshire. Private, modest and independent in spirit, Marriott delighted in the restoration and care of the Grimston estate for the rest of his life.

Oliver Digby James Marriott was born in West Sussex on August 27 1939, one of four children of Rowland Marriott and his wife Evelyn, née Caillard.

Descended from a line of learned Leicestershire clergymen (though there were also ancestral connections in Yorkshire), Oliver was educated at Eton but shunned the family tradition of going on to study at Oxford. Instead, he became a clerk in an aggregates company in Pimlico and later with Rothschilds in the City.

In 1961 he was victorious as a Labour candidate for Lutterworth Rural District Council in Leicestershire, becoming the youngest councillor in the country. But he was bored with his banking job – and in the same year he discovered his true métier when he joined Investors’ Chronicle under the wing of its pioneering property editors Charles Gordon and Gary Arnott.

After a stint on The Statist magazine, he moved in 1965 to The Sunday Times (where the then deputy editor William Rees-Mogg gave him a month off to finish the property book) and was financial editor of The Times from 1967 to 1969.

In his later career, Marriott was founder chairman of two smaller property companies, Churchbury Estates and Ilex, and a non-executive director of Shaftesbury, which owned tracts of Soho and Covent Garden.

A committed Eurosceptic, he stood in the 2005 general election as the Ukip candidate for the Conservative-held Beverley and Holderness constituency, attracting just 4.7 per cent of the poll with 2,336 votes.

His older brother Richard, a City financier who was Lord-Lieutenant of East Yorkshire, restored another historic house, Boynton Hall, a few miles north of Grimston; the brothers shared a particular love of books.

Passionate in his devotion to family life, Oliver Marriott married, in 1964, Lif Kristina Rolandsdottir from Stockholm, who survives him with their two daughters.

​Oliver Marriott, born August 27 1939, died December 5 2025
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Starmer’s rail nationalisation risks returning Britain to 1970s, warns Labour MP
data.description:
Sir Keir Starmer’s blueprint for nationalising the railway risks dragging the network back to the 1970s, one of his own MPs has warned.

Scott Arthur, a member of the Commons transport select committee, said that making Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, the ultimate arbiter in disputes concerning Great British Railways (GBR), could politicise the most minor decisions.

He said that would prompt scrutiny on a par with British Rail (BR) in the 1970s, when even the state of its notoriously unpalatable sandwiches became the subject of political debate.

Mr Arthur, the Labour MP for Edinburgh South West, said at a meeting of the select committee: “For GBR to succeed it has to have some independence from the Government, it has to be at arm’s length.

“With the Secretary of State having so much discretion and so much input over it, is there not a danger that it’s just going to become politicised?

“We’re going to end up having urgent statements on how fresh the sandwiches are on the railway like we used to do back in the old days.”

Sandwiches sold on British Rail services in the 1970s, often served soggy after being bound in cling film, were a political football and staple of comedy shows for decades.

The sandwiches became a source of frustration for British Rail, generating more headlines than innovations such as the High Speed Train.

Prue Leith was hired to oversee an overhaul in 1977, but the sandwiches still featured prominently in a privatisation debate a decade later, with then Tory MP Teresa Gorman calling for one to be preserved as a symbol of the state-run railway.

Mr Arthur told MPs on Wednesday that handing powers currently held by the Office of Rail and Road to the Transport Secretary also risked undermining public confidence in nationalisation.

Rail-freight firms and the handful of private passenger operators that will carry on providing so-called open access services have claimed that GBR will inevitably favour its own services when allocating paths for trains, and that Ms Alexander is likely to back its decisions.

Mr Arthur said: “The Secretary of State is accumulating all these powers. As a member of the public, there’s no transparency there if I’m concerned about the decision-making process.

“There’s no way for me to understand how decisions are reached if it’s the Secretary of State that’s guiding the process and she’s one of the partners within the process.”

He said that while some people might be “excited to hear the ORR will be monitoring the situation,” the public would prefer it to have “more teeth.”

Mr Arthur said he was also concerned that Ms Alexander would be able slash targets for boosting freight operations at her own discretion, and questioned why the railways bill currently passing through Parliament includes no targets for passenger numbers.

He said: “Is there not going to be a temptation that to reduce the cost to the taxpayer they simply reduce services and end up with fewer passengers on the railway?”

Lord Hendy, the rail minister, said the idea that the Government would become more hands on following nationalisation was “fatuous” and that once train and track operations were united under GBR, he intended to be less closely involved with day-to-day matters.

He told the committee: “I don’t want to answer these letters about why the 8.24am from Richmond didn’t run this morning. I want them to be answered by someone who is actually responsible.

“The people running GBR will have nowhere to go because GBR will be responsible for everything. I want them to wake up and say, ‘This is all my fault and we have got to fix it.’”

He said the railway had even proved “incapable” of implementing a new timetable on the line from London Kings Cross to Edinburgh despite £4bn of investment, forcing him to take on a duty that would fall to a minister nowhere else “outside North Korea and the old USSR”.

data.title: The Traitors producer in talks to merge  with studio behind Peaky Blinders
data.description:
The production companies behind Peaky Blinders and The Traitors are in talks to merge after abandoning a push to buy ITV’s studio division.

Banijay, the French company behind Peaky Blinders, the Birmingham-set gangster drama, as well as MasterChef, is said to be in discussions to merge with Traitors-maker All3Media, which is owned by RedBird IMI.

If confirmed, a tie-up would create one of the world’s largest production groups with operations spanning Europe. The joint entity would have around £5bn in revenues, according to the latest available accounts.

All3Media, which also makes Gogglebox, was Britain’s largest independent producer prior to its £1.2bn sale to RedBird IMI three years ago. The United Arab Emirates-backed (UAE) fund made an unsuccessful swoop for The Telegraph last year.

Banijay is headquartered in France and counts French billionaires Bernard Arnault and Vincent Bolloré among its shareholders.

Both sides previously explored a merger in 2023 when All3Media was put up for sale by previous owners Liberty Global and Warner Bros Discovery. Banijay pulled out of the running and All3Media was subsequently sold to RedBird IMI.

The potential merger would have implications for ITV, which is currently embroiled in its own merger activity.

Banijay and All3Media last year separately explored a £3bn takeover bid for ITV Studios, the broadcaster’s production arm.

ITV Studios is set to be spun out as a stand-alone business if ITV succeeds in selling its broadcasting business to Comcast, the US owner of Sky.

If Banijay and All3Media merge, the combined entity may be forced to ditch its aspirations for ITV Studios because they will need to focus on integrating the businesses.

As a result, ITV Studios may be forced to forge its own combination with another producer such as Fremantle, which is owned by German media conglomerate Bertelsmann.

The radical redrawing of the global media industry’s boundaries comes as traditional broadcasters and producers seek scale to compete with now-dominant streaming rivals such as Netflix and Disney.

Netflix is itself in a battle to take control of Warner Bros, in a deal that would mark a significant reshaping of Hollywood.

Paramount is seeking to gatecrash the deal with its own $108bn (£80bn) bid, which is backed by RedBird IMI’s US partner RedBird Capital.

Production groups are also seeking to team up as streaming services seek to sign bigger deals with a smaller number of suppliers.

Any deal would probably involve the smaller All3Media injecting money into the combined company, according to Reuters, which first reported the discussions.

Banijay, All3Media and RedBird IMI declined to comment.

data.title: BP reveals £3.7bn hit from botched net zero transition
data.description:
BP faces a $5bn (£3.7bn) hit following its failed embrace of net zero.

The oil and gas giant revealed the impairment in a trading update on Wednesday morning, prompting shares to fall as much as 1pc in early trading.

The company told shareholders the losses were “primarily attributable to the gas and low carbon energy segment”.

The update comes as BP is ditching its expensive bet on net zero following a backlash from shareholders.

Bernard Looney, the former chief executive, set out plans in 2020 to slash the company’s oil output by 40pc and reach net zero carbon emissions by 2050 in a break from the rest of the industry.

However, the pivot to green energy proved disastrous, with BP’s share price underperforming rivals. The company has been incrementally gutting Mr Looney’s strategy since he left in 2023.

BP has halted plans for biofuel refineries planned in Rotterdam, in the Netherlands, and Lingen, in north-west Germany. It has also sold off 10 US onshore windfarms, frozen offshore wind investments and announced plans to sell most of its stake in solar developer Lightsource.

In the UK, the company has also abandoned planned investments in hydrogen production plants.

Maurizio Carulli, a global energy analyst at Quilter Cheviot, said the impairments were “a legacy of BP’s previous controversial strategic shift towards renewables”.

Last month BP unexpectedly ousted Murray Auchincloss, the chief executive, after less than two years in the job. He was replaced by Meg O’Neill, the company’s first ever female boss.

Ms O’Neill is renowned for taking a tough line on climate-related issues and previously had a long career at the US oil giant ExxonMobil. Earlier this year, she attacked young people who criticise the fossil fuel industry, suggesting they were hypocritical.

Her appointment comes as Albert Manifold, BP’s new chairman, seeks to quicken the company’s refocusing on oil and drive a turnaround. Activist investors such as Elliott Management had criticised progress under Mr Auchincloss as too slow.

Ms O’Neill will become BP’s third chief executive in two and a half years when she takes over in April.

BP said on Wednesday that it expected to book a $4bn to $5bn impairment in its so-called transition businesses.

While the company is pivoting back to fossil fuels, BP on Wednesday joined FTSE 100 rival Shell in cautioning over weaker performance from oil trading amid a drop in the price of crude at the end of last year.

However, it said further progress had been made on slashing debts. Net debt fell to between $22bn and $23bn at the end of last year, down from $26.1bn at the end of September.

BP will publish its fourth quarter results on Feb 10.

data.title: Goldman Sachs traders eye push into gambling on prediction markets
data.description:
Goldman Sachs traders are eyeing a push into the world of gambling on prediction markets.

David Solomon, the bank’s chief executive, said he had met with the bosses of major prediction markets in recent weeks and wanted to understand the opportunities on offer.

Traders are eyeing a push into the space after an explosion in their popularity over the last year.

Prediction markets allow individuals to bet on the outcomes of events as diverse as whether the US would bomb Iran to who would win the Golden Globes. The two largest markets in the US are Kalshi and Polymarket, which accepts bets in cryptocurrency.

Mr Solomon said: “I personally met with the two big prediction companies and their leadership in the last two weeks and spent a couple of hours with each to learn more about that. 

“We have a team of people here that are spending time with them and are looking at it.”

Polymarket was banned from operating in the US under the Biden administration, however, Donald Trump has lifted restrictions as part of a broader embrace of the crypto market.

The platform rose to prominence after users correctly predicted Mr Trump’s resounding win in the 2024 election, despite pollsters predicting a much tighter race.

Kalshi, a similar platform, was created by Tarek Mansour, who came up with the concept while working as an equity derivatives intern at Goldman Sachs in 2016.

At the time, institutions were scrambling to guard against the fallout from Brexit, giving Mr Mansour the idea of an exchange to trade events themselves rather than financial assets.

While these markets effectively amount to gambling on the outcome of world events, they are considered to be financial derivative products in the US and are regulated in the same way as futures or options contracts that Goldman and other banks already trade.

‘Opportunity’ to scale

Mr Solomon’s comments came as Goldman Sachs retook its crown as the best bank on Wall Street for stock trading after a record $4.3bn (£3.2bn) haul for the final three months of last year.

The profit from trading was almost $700m higher than what analysts had expected. It ended Morgan Stanley’s three-month spell as the top stock trading bank on Wall Street, a spot that Goldman had previously held since the end of 2022.

On a busy day for results in Wall Street, Goldman and its rival Morgan Stanley both reported a rise in profits in the fourth quarter.

Mr Solomon added that corporate bosses are trying to use the next three years of the Trump administration to pursue major deals.

He said: “I think CEOs and boards are looking and saying, OK, we’ve got a window here of a handful of years where the opportunity to consider big, strategic, transformative things is certainly possible.

“CEOs definitely believe that the art of the deal and scaled consolidation is possible now.”

BlackRock, the world’s biggest asset manager, also broke records on Thursday as it reported assets under management of over $14tn for the first time.

It pulled in $342bn of new client money, beating Wall Street estimates. Of this, $12.7bn came from private markets.

data.title: Thames Water’s rescue plan risks setting a dangerous precedent
data.description:
The latest version of a creditor-led rescue plan for Thames Water ticks multiple boxes. One can see why the company, ministers and the industry regulator might be minded to give it the green light in the coming weeks.

The pressure to hammer out a deal is intense. The threat of a multibillion-pound bill landing on Rachel Reeves’s desk before her Budget in November is concentrating Whitehall minds.

Thames is burning cash rapidly. A separate announcement from the company revealed that in August it called on the remaining £628m of a £1.5bn emergency loan.

It is further worrying evidence of the extent to which an organisation providing critical services to 13 million people continues to live hand-to-mouth after years of reckless ownership.

With the clock ticking, the “London and Valley” consortium of lenders has come up with a decent upgrade on the somewhat vague proposal published a month ago.

Then, the prospective new owners declared their undying commitment to “a new transparent and collaborative relationship with regulators”, before outlining a plan lacking in details.

The highlights of their latest attempt include a more generous portion of equity and a bigger debt write-off than previously envisaged. A commitment to not pay dividends for five years, along with the ambition of eventually returning Thames to the stock market are also welcome measures.

Yet all the hard work that has gone into answering many of the concerns of regulators, Government and long-suffering customers risks being overshadowed by what amounts to a fresh attempt to wriggle out of fines.

Having sensibly given up on trying to dodge outstanding penalties for pumping sewage into waterways, Thames’s wannabe owners are now trying to inoculate themselves against future punishment.

“The current regulatory framework for the water industry does not allow it to successfully turnaround its business,” London and Valley says.

The consortium won’t take “no” for an answer, it seems.

Steve Reed, the former environment secretary, told parliament in June that Thames must meet its statutory obligations, after being asked about possible “regulatory easements”.

But the very idea that an industry that treats Britain’s rivers as its dumping ground should face softer environmental targets – or “stretching but achievable incentives” in the consortium’s words – won’t wash.

It’s hard to square this with the consortium’s warm words.

Mike McTighe, the proposed future chairman of Thames – he is also chairman of the company behind The Telegraph – talks about “rebuilding public trust”.

The creditors themselves admit that Thames has presided over “a cycle of underinvestment and underperformance that has driven trust in Thames Water to an all-time low.”

Few would argue with that assessment. Yet any agreement that makes it easier for the company to pollute with greater impunity is an improbable basis for the restoration of Thames’s relationship with the public.

Consider that in an industry that has vandalised the environment, this is a chronic offender.

Thames’s record is getting worse, not better. Some 50pc more raw sewage was discharged into Britain’s waterways from Thames’s ageing system last year, compared with the previous 12 months.

In May, Thames Water was fined £123m by Ofwat – the biggest ever fine imposed by the regulator.

From a purely financial perspective, the creditors’ plea for clemency is understandable.

They expect future fines of more than £1bn if current regulations are applied and argue that something has to give if Thames is to receive the investment it needs.

This financial hole was of Thames’s own making, however. Its creditors bankrolled it as it hollowed itself out. To turn around now and demand that the rules are changed to make pumping effluent into rivers a less costly business is an affront to justice and risks setting a horrible precedent.

Perhaps even now, after months and months of negotiations, the hedge funds that swooped in to buy up debt on the cheap when Thames was on the brink, still don’t understand the extent of public and political anger.

Customer ire stretches to the whole gamut of misdeeds – rising bills, poor service, undeserved dividends and excessive executive pay.

But it is the epidemic of pollution that has attracted the most fury. That the creditors have chosen to attack on that front seems “courageous”, as they say in the Civil Service.

Indeed, I am willing to bet that the majority of people want to see stiffer fines, not softer ones, particularly if the current set-up has failed to act as a deterrent. Weaker rules would surely lead to more pollution, not less.

Meanwhile, the knock-on effect of granting Thames an easier ride would be that every other water company in Britain would be entitled to expect the same exemptions on the basis of fairness.

A reminder from the creditors that their offer is the only credible one left after private equity firm KKR abandoned its bid looks designed to focus minds. Ditto talks of how the plan “will avoid special administration”, and ensure that “Thames Water’s turnaround will not fall to the UK taxpayer”.

Decency, however, demands that Ofwat and the Government don’t allow themselves to be pushed around by a bunch of opportunistic hedge funds.

data.title: Diesel lorries to be banned in eco drive
data.description:
Labour has unveiled plans to introduce a petrol and diesel lorry ban as part of their net-zero drive.

Ministers have announced an end to the sale of new fossil fuel-powered trucks in a move that opens up a new battle with the Tories and Reform.

They have ruled out allowing the continued use of low-carbon or synthetic fuels, meaning that from 2040 all new heavy goods vehicles (HGVs) will have to be electric.

Critics warned that forcing haulage companies to adopt expensive battery or hydrogen-powered vehicles would push up the cost of business.

The extra expense would then be passed onto consumers in the form of higher prices for goods like food and clothes, as well as parcel deliveries.

Richard Holden, the Tory shadow transport secretary, said: “Labour are sleepwalking into a cost shock for the entire economy.

“Forcing haulage firms to scrap perfectly good lorries while they can still run for years and replace them with other vehicles before either the technology or infrastructure is ready will simply drive up costs, as well as being environmentally disastrous.

“Those costs will be passed straight through supply chains into higher prices in shops, higher construction costs, and more pressure on inflation. Every supermarket shelf, building site, and home delivery will feel higher prices.

“Rather than letting innovation and the market cut emissions sensibly, Labour are reaching straight for quotas, caps and bans. Central planning with a delivery charge attached is delivering another blow to the British economy.”

The move risks exposing Labour to further attacks from the Tories and Reform, who have both pledged to roll back costly net zero policies.

Both parties have said they would scrap the 2030 petrol car ban and cut subsidies, which would cost taxpayers billions a year.

The 2040 date for phasing out petrol trucks was first proposed by the Tories in November 2021 at the height of Boris Johnson’s net zero ambitions.

In a consultation document published last week, the Department for Transport set out three options for enforcing the new petrol lorry ban.

The first would see manufacturers set annual quotas for the number of electric HGVs they must sell, similar to how the petrol car ban will work.

Another option laid out by ministers is to follow the EU’s approach and set haulage companies ever decreasing carbon emissions caps.

Finally, delivery firms could be required to ensure that an ever increasing proportion of their fleet was electric, eventually reaching 100 per cent.

Chris Ashley, from the Road Haulage Association (RHA), said: “We’re clear that the decarbonisation transition hinges on two key issues being addressed – infrastructure in place to power zero emission HGVs, and vehicle affordability.

“The pathway to decarbonise lorries and coaches in the UK must be realistic and viable – we’ll look to see how the consultation achieves that.”

In a survey run by the RHA last year, seven in 10 haulage firms said they had no plans to add any zero-emission vehicles to their fleets. The main barriers cited were the extra costs involved in buying electric-powered lorries and concerns over their lack of range.

Figures from February last year show that there were just 500 electric HGVs registered in the UK out of a total fleet of 535,000.

Extra subsidies needed

Separately, the Green Finance Institute has estimated £100bn worth of additional finance and subsidies would be needed to support electric lorry take-up.

More than 80 per cent of goods are transported around Britain by road, meaning an increase in cost for hauliers could have a significant impact on prices.

The logistics sector also employs almost 2.7 million workers, with its more than 200,000 businesses adding £185bn to the UK economy every year.

For smaller trucks up to 26 tonnes, the sale of new petrol and diesel-powered models will be banned from as early as 2035 under the plans.

Keir Mather, the minister for transport decarbonisation, said the Government was aware it would be “challenging” to decarbonise the sector. But he insisted there was a need to act because lorries “have a high environmental impact” and emit 16 per cent of all emissions from domestic transport.

“That is why we are firmly committed to accelerating the transition to clean technologies and to setting a path to phase out the sale of…all new non-zero emission heavy goods vehicles by 2040,” he wrote.

He claimed technological advances would lead to “ever better, cheaper and more capable vehicles” that would ultimately bring down costs for haulage firms.

data.title: How the 50/30/20 budgeting hack can unlock your finances
data.description:
Budgeting is the key to running your finances smoothly. Whether you have a high or low income – or somewhere in between – a budget is important to maintain the levels of spending and saving you can afford.

Getting a handle on your income and outgoings can help reset your finances and spending patterns by assessing where your money goes every month.

A budget can also be a real help in achieving specific financial goals you might have. This could be saving for a summer holiday or a new car, or raising a deposit for a house, a wedding, or even paying off debts.

There are many different budgeting methods to choose from.

The 50/30/20 rule is a straightforward budgeting method that can help you to manage your money simply and effectively so you live within your means and reach your goals – whether that’s to spend less to pay off debts or to save more for your future.

How the 50/30/20 rule works

This method of budgeting suggests dividing your monthly income into three spending categories: 50pc for “needs”, 30pc for “wants” and 20pc for “financial goals”.

The 50pc towards needs will be the portion that covers all the regular essential outgoings such as your mortgage or rent, and household bills such as gas and electricity, water, mobile phone, house insurance, as well as the cost of running a car if you have one.

You would then set aside 30pc for wants, which pays for things like hobbies and sports, socialising, shopping and subscriptions for TV, music streaming or newspapers and magazines.

The remaining 20pc can be used for financial goals, such as paying off debt, contributing to savings, making longer-term investments or saving into a pension.

The 50/30/20 rule can work, in theory, whatever your income level.

Someone earning £34,000 (around the UK national average) would take home £28,000 a year or £2,333 a month, according to calculations by Hargreaves Lansdown, an investment platform.

According to the 50/30/20 rule, this would give you £1,166 a month to spend on needs, £700 on wants, and £467 on savings. With a 5pc pension contribution – which is £142 a month – that would leave you £325 to put aside in the savings pot.

A higher earner on a salary of £60,000 would take home £46,357 a year, or £3,780 a month.

It would give you £1,890 a month to spend on needs, £1,134 on wants and £756 on savings. A 5pc pension contribution – which is £250 a month – means you’re left with £506 to save or repay debts.

If you had a credit card debt of £5,000, you would be able to clear it between 13 and 16 months if you used the budgeting rule.

Equally, this amount could go towards your £20,000 annual tax-free Isa allowance.

Sarah Coles, head of personal finance at Hargreaves Lansdown, said: “How you use the savings money will depend on your circumstances.

“Some will need to pay down expensive short-term debts, while others will want to build emergency savings, their pension or investments – often people will want to do all of these things, so will split the cash between the different goals.”

Bending the budgeting rules

One big problem with this method is that it may not be possible to get all your essential bills into the 50pc bracket.

Ms Coles said: “The proportions won’t work for everyone, largely because the cost of putting a roof over your head is so high in the UK.”

Ms Coles calculated that if you took a middle-income household that earns £32,651 after tax – or £2,721 a month – the cost of their essentials is £1,755 a month, which is 65pc of their income.

If you then put 30pc aside for wants, you’re left with next to nothing to save for the future.

It means you need to flex your “wants” to fit the cash available, and free up enough money for the future, which might work out as 65pc needs, 15pc wants and 20pc savings.

To cut the cost of your “wants”, you’ll need to come up with some sacrifices.

Cutting out one of the restaurant meals you have each month can mean money is redirected to the needs pot, for example. You might also need to ruthlessly cut shopping trips and subscriptions to achieve your goals.

If that doesn’t work for you, you may find that other breakdowns are more achievable – and these could change during the year as life dictates different spending patterns.

Ms Coles added: “Simple rules of thumb like the 50/30/20 can be useful for keeping you roughly on track. However, it’s not a good idea to try to follow them without calculating whether they’ll work for you, or they could lead you astray.”

Timeless budgeting tips

Sticking to a budget can be tricky, and if you’ve already tried a method that didn’t work for you it can be easy to give up.

Budgeting isn’t a one-size-fits-all situation. If you don’t think you can stick to such a strict regime such as the 50/30/20 rule, some of these key budgeting tips can help you stay in control of your money.

1. Understand where your money goes

You might roughly know what you spend your money on each month, but it will help to know your exact outgoings – and what they’re spent on.

If you don’t review your spending often, one way to do this is to go through your bank statements either online or on a banking app and make a list of the bills you pay as well as amounts for online subscriptions, eating out, travel, supermarket shopping, clothes, sports and other treats.

The list might be long, but you can then see where your earnings go – and where any savings can be made.

Many banking apps will automatically categorise your spending so you can easily see whether a large proportion of your money goes on eating out.

If yours doesn’t, then apps such as Moneyhub, Snoop and Emma can do it for you.

2. Know your goals

Each of us has different goals and things we want to save up for – whether it’s a holiday, home improvements or a wedding – or debts to pay off.

Keeping these in mind when thinking about your spending and saving means you have a better chance of reaching them.

3. ‘No spend’ weekends

When an unexpected expense comes your way, such as a car repair or faulty boiler, you may feel the pinch.

A no-spend weekend can be a great way to offset any overspending you’ve done. It involves going 48 hours without tapping any card or buying online. It’s not necessarily about giving up things you enjoy doing, but rather an opportunity to get creative with ways to have fun for free.

This can also be a way of preparing for an expense, boosting your savings.

For an even bigger challenge, you could join the people making “no spend” months popular.

4. Be open about your money mission

Tell friends and family if you’re on a strict money diet so they can support you.

This tactic – also known as “loud budgeting” – can hopefully mean you won’t be embarrassed to opt out of expensive dinners out and any other pricey get-togethers, as you’ll have already made it clear that you’re keeping your spending to a minimum.

5. Seek help if you need it

Some restructuring of your borrowing might be enough to help you regain control.

But if this and some smart budgeting aren’t enough to resolve your money problems, then don’t shy away from seeking help.

Debt advice is best sought from a debt charity, which will advise you, and act on your behalf, with nothing to pay for their services.

Help from a debt charity will have an immediate benefit as they can often negotiate freezing interest payments on some debts to give you some breathing space while you work out a plan for the road ahead.

Beware of debt help or loan consolidation companies that market their services heavily. They will often charge for advice and these fees will only serve to add to your existing debts.

Try the National Debtline (0808 808 4000), StepChange Debt Charity (0800 138 1111), or the Debt Advice Foundation (0800 043 40 50).
