data.title: Millions of households to face higher water bills
data.description:
Millions of households are facing higher-than-expected water bills after the competition watchdog allowed five utility companies to increase their charges.

Following a provisional ruling by the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA), Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, South East Water, Southern Water and Wessex Water will be able to increase bills by a collective £556m over the next five years.

While that is less than the £2.7bn requested by the companies, it will still lead to some households paying annual bills of up to £638 a year.

Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, said she understood the public’s anger over bill rises, but expected companies to offer support to those struggling to pay.

Between them, Anglian and Northumbrian have been allowed to raise bills by a further 1pc, while Southern, South East and Wessex have been handed increases of 3pc, 4pc and 5pc, respectively.

Together, they serve around seven million customers across the UK.

The ruling means that Anglian will charge an average bill of £599, while Northumbrian bill payers will pay £495. South East Water will increase bills to £322, while Southern will charge £638.

Wessex was handed the biggest increase of 5pc, meaning its bills will rise to £622. All had asked for larger increases.

Appeal to the CMA

It comes after the companies appealed to the CMA earlier this year, requesting permission to increase their bills higher than Ofwat, the water regulator, had previously ruled.

In defence of its decision, the CMA said the extra money would help reduce pollution and strengthen supply, although it also said it would help cover the companies’ rising debt costs.

Kirstin Baker, who chaired an independent group of experts appointed by the CMA, said: “We’ve found that water companies’ requests for significant bill increases, on top of those allowed by Ofwat, are largely unjustified.

“We understand the real pressure on household budgets and have worked to keep increases to a minimum, while still ensuring there is funding to deliver essential improvements at a reasonable cost.”

Thames Water, Britain’s biggest water company, had also appealed, but its proceedings have been put on hold while it battles to stave off nationalisation.

Ms Hardy said efforts were being made to restore the public’s trust in water companies.

She said: “We’ve made sure that investment cash goes into infrastructure upgrades, not bonuses, and we’re creating a tough new regulator to clean up our waterways and restore trust in the system.”

It comes amid growing anger over the state of the water sector, which has been responsible for a rise in sewage failures in recent years.

Mike Keil, the chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), said: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.

“CCW’s own analysis suggests there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs, cutting bills by around £41 a year, but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return.

“There is a danger the customers of these companies will end up paying more, without seeing any additional improvements in return.”

Joanna Elson, the chief executive of Independent Age, a charity, said higher bills would be “extremely damaging to people on low incomes living in these areas”.

She said: “The people in later life we support tell us the drastic lengths they are taking to reduce their water costs, including only washing a couple of times a week, not flushing the toilet and cutting down on laundry. This shouldn’t be happening in modern Britain.”

Ms Elson added: “We urge the UK Government to protect customers on low incomes from rising water bills by establishing a national water social tariff. This will end the postcode lottery.

 “It’s not right that your address dictates how much financial support you receive.”

data.title: One in four electric cars sold in Britain are Chinese
data.description:
More than one in four electric vehicles (EVs) sold in the UK are now made in China, as Beijing helps to power Britain’s shift away from petrol and diesel.

Chinese-made vehicles accounted for 27.9pc of the more than 470,000 electric cars sold in the UK last year, according to the Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders (SMMT).

Across all fuel types, Chinese-made vehicles accounted for a record 13.5pc of the total market last year, or one in eight. Sales rose by more than 50pc amid an aggressive push by brands including BYD, Jaecoo and Omoda to break into the UK.

Sales of BYD cars grew more than fivefold in Britain last year. The Chinese giant overtook Tesla to become the world’s biggest seller of electric cars in 2025.

Even celebrated British brands such as MG now count as made in China after falling under foreign ownership. Sweden-headquartered EV brand Polestar also manufacture in the country, while some Tesla models are made at the company’s Shanghai plant.

The surge in Chinese EV sales helped electric vehicles to account for 23.4pc of new cars registered in Britain in 2025, rising to 32.3pc in December, the SMMT said.

The figure will be welcomed by Labour ministers, who have vowed to ban the sale of new petrol and diesel cars by 2030 and hybrids by 2035.

However, the growing reliance on China for EVs is likely to prove controversial. Both the European Union and US have cracked down on imports from China, amid concerns about unfair subsidies and possible national security threats.

The US-based Centre for Strategic and International Studies has estimated that the Chinese government spent at least $230bn (£170bn) between 2009 to 2023 supporting its EV industry.

A 100pc tariff on Chinese electric vehicles in the US has effectively closed the market, while the EU has imposed heavy tariffs on imports.

The UK Government has said it has no plans for tariffs on Chinese car imports.

When combined with hybrid vehicles, battery-powered cars now account for just under half of all new car sales, the SMMT said. Plug-in hybrids, which combine a smaller battery with a petrol engine, are the fastest-growing part of the market, rising by 35pc last year.

Sales of fully electric cars were up 24pc, while petrol and diesel cars fell 8pc and 15pc respectively.

However, sales of EVs remained behind the zero emission vehicle mandate, which required 28pc of cars sold in 2025 to be electric.

This means sales were even further behind target than in 2024, when EVs made up 19.6pc of sales compared to a 22pc target. The mandate will rise again this year to a third of total car sales.

Manufacturers who miss the quota can cover the difference by buying credits from those who surpass it, making up sales in later years, or by cutting the CO2 emissions of other cars on the road.

However, manufacturers that are significantly below-target face the prospect of paying a fee of £12,000 per non-compliant car sold.

The SMMT said that manufacturers spent £5.5bn last year on subsidising EV sales in order to come closer to the target, or £11,000 per vehicle. The trade body said this was “unsustainable” and urged ministers to ease the requirements.

“The mandate still challenges the industry to hit levels that are apart from the natural demand that’s there,” said Mike Hawes, the SMMT’s chief executive.

He said a review of the mandate scheduled for 2027 should be brought forward to this year. “The assumptions behind the mandate when they were made, we need to review them.”

The EU has delayed plans to ban combustion engine cars, moving the date from 2035 to 2040. Labour has resisted calls to follow suit.

Overall, sales of new cars rose 3.5pc to 2.02m last year, the highest level since 2019, although they remain some way below pre-Covid levels.

data.title: ‘Un-pensioning’ could set a very dangerous precedent indeed
data.description:
Join Michael in the comment section below from 11am.

At least 150 teachers have been banned from schools over the last 10 years for child sexual abuse.

The true figure could be considerably higher as Freedom of Information Act requests show that since 2015 a further 256 were struck off for non-defined sexual misconduct and another 245 for “breach of boundaries/trust”.

Yet all these nearly 700 former teachers – indeed all 1,095 teachers who have been disbarred for whatever reason since 2015 – have retained whatever pension entitlements they previously accrued.

The power exists to strip teachers of their pension if they commit treason (thankfully still not the most common of occurrences), serious violations of the Official Secrets Act (there will be little in the way of classified information lying around most schools) or offences committed in connection with their role that could damage confidence in the profession or are “gravely injurious to the state”.

But this power has not been exercised in recent decades. No teacher has lost their pension since at least 2005. The power is also rarely used in relation to others in the public sector – under the NHS pension scheme just 33 people have been stripped of their entitlements since the 1970s.

Now, victims groups are calling for this to change and for those who have so despicably breached the trust placed in them to lose their pensions.

One must have sympathy with this line of argument.

Few sane people would wish to stand up for the rights of paedophiles. Those who abuse children in their care fully deserve to be punished to the full extent the law permits.

There is a very strong case for awarding the pensions of abusers to their victims. Such actions are not just criminal, they are also civil wrongs for which victims are entitled to sue for compensation and seize assets from their abusers.

But in terms of the state stripping people of their pension entitlements, let us be careful what we wish for.

This is a dangerous road to go down, especially in the hands of our current Labour Government and the woke educational and NHS blob. What starts with a clampdown on sex abusers could soon end up with public sector workers losing their pensions for wrong-speak. The legions of the debanked – almost half a million customers last year – could then be joined by the unpensioned.

This is not an argument about the sustainability of public sector pensions and whether the current arrangements can be justified. The Teachers’ Pension Scheme costs taxpayers around £9bn per year. The total liability of public sector pensions is estimated at £5.8tn.

If all these schemes were closed now, annual costs would still rise for decades – peaking at £132bn in 2061.

The whole system is in urgent need of reform, but that is a completely different issue from individuals losing their entitlements for misconduct. In addition, that public sector pensions are such a costly behemoth is not the fault of individual teachers, nurses or doctors. It is a consequence of decades of cowardice by politicians of all parties, finding it easier to avoid having to take difficult decisions.

The reason why unpensioning miscreants is generally wrong, I would argue, is that don’t imagine if ministers start to get a taste for such action they will stop with such offenders. Again and again, policies which have started as a means of combatting a genuine issue have morphed into a much wider bureaucratic power grab.

Just take a look at the Prevent programme at schools, set up to combat Islamist extremism. Examining the scheme now, you might imagine that the biggest domestic threat to British national security did not come from Islamic State adherents and their fellow travellers, but from the extreme Right.

In the year to the end of March 2024, 45pc of referrals were for “extreme Right concerns”, almost double the 23pc referred for “Islamist concerns”. Another 18pc were referred for the rather catch all-sounding “conflicted ideology”.

The case against unpensioning people we wholly disapprove of is the same as why we should champion free speech for those with views we abhor.

Standing up for the rights of people we broadly agree with to express their views is simple. The real test is whether we are willing to stand up for the rights of those whose views we hate.

It is the easiest thing in the world for someone of my world view to be outraged if a university bans a speaker for their Brexit-y, gender critical or pro-Israel views. It is much more difficult – and for that reason all the more important – to stand up for freedom of expression for trans activists and those taking, say, a virulent anti-Israel stance.

If we clamour for the unpensioning of those who are genuinely reprehensible, we will have handed another tool to the Whitehall machine to regulate behaviour they don’t approve of. And who knows who will be next in their sights.

By past performance, it could well be those expressing views which run counter to the prevailing consensus. And those views will generally be of a conservative persuasion.

data.title: Leon boss attacks Labour’s ‘toxic’ tax regime
data.description:
Labour is overseeing a “toxic” tax regime that threatens swathes of closures across the hospitality industry, the founder of Leon has said.

John Vincent said soaring costs meant that almost all pubs and restaurants are losing money and he claimed that the only places that will survive are those “selling c--p food”.

The chief executive of the fast-food chain criticised the Government after he recently unveiled plans to shut 20 restaurants as part of a radical turnaround plan.

Mr Vincent said 36p in every pound was going to the Government under the “incredibly toxic tax regime that’s impacting our industry”, leaving Leon with “negative 10p” after expenses as it battles higher National Insurance costs and rising business rates.

“You go to a supermarket, you buy something, you don’t pay VAT,” he told the BBC’s Big Boss Interview podcast. “You go to Leon, you pay 20pc VAT.

“The second thing is you have business rates that were 4pc of sales and they are now 8pc to 10pc of sales.

“You have National Insurance that’s now gone up an extra 3pc of sales. In our industry, you only ever made 3pc profit after depreciation and after head office costs.

“So if your business rates go up from 4pc to 8pc or 10pc and the National Insurance is added, everyone starts losing money.

“The only people that are going to survive are those that are selling c--p food that’s not very good quality because that’s the only way that these restaurants are going to be able to survive.”

Mr Vincent bought back Leon in October. He founded the business in 2004 but later sold it to petrol station tycoons Mohsin and Zuber Issa in 2021, who also bought supermarket Asda around the same time.

Mr Vincent said Leon had “lost chutzpah, leadership and confidence” under the Issas, who turned the fast food chain into an “Asda business”.

“Those businesses are typically run for money and my really strong belief is that when you run a business for money, certainly a business like Leon, you end up making less money,” he said.

“If you focus on soul, purpose, then product, then business model, then people, then customer, then economics, the economics become good.”

Leon hired administrators last month to oversee a Company Voluntary Arrangement (CVA), a restructuring programme that will allow the business to push through restaurant closures.

Mr Vincent co-founded Leon in 2004 with Henry Dimbleby and Allegra McEvedy. It was set up as a healthier alternative to fast food. Its success led the Government to task Mr Dimbleby with leading a national review of the food system in 2019.
